full_text:
**Protocol for organoid imaging and charting with ImageJ & Excel**

1. On the final day of culturing (before fixation), take pictures of
 > 3-4 wells of each condition with the EVOS microscope (10x
 > magnification). It is good to take several pictures in order to
 > make sure that we get pictures of sufficient quality for
 > analyzing.

> Label each picture with all necessary information, ex.
> S034_pAdn_ome_p0_d7_10x.

2. Import the images to your computer and open them in ImageJ/Fiji. Set
 > the scale in ImageJ according to the 10x magnification of the
 > images as follows:

> ![Kuva, joka sisältää kohteen näyttökuva Kuvaus luotu
> automaattisesti](media/image2.png){width="2.209086832895888in"
> height="2.2252701224846896in"}
>
> (This scaling was determined according to information found on
> http://microscopynotes.com/evos/.)

a. Analyze -\> Set scale

b. Set scale (known distance) based on this table

c. ![](media/image1.png){width="2.7682819335083115in"
 > height="1.8543088363954505in"}

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
3. Analyze each clear organoid from each image/field using the
 > oval/elliptical/freehand selections tools. Choose the tool based
 > on the organoid shape and define each organoid as accurately as
 > possible. When you have circled the whole organoid, go to Analyze
 > 🡪 Tools 🡪 ROI manager and choose Add \[t\]. This will add the
 > organoid to a list.

4.

id as accurately as
 > possible. When you have circled the whole organoid, go to Analyze
 > 🡪 Tools 🡪 ROI manager and choose Add \[t\]. This will add the
 > organoid to a list.

4. Select the organoid you're working with from the list and rename it.
 > Use numbers starting from 1 for each image/field, this allows you
 > to easily determine the sum of organoids as well.

> (By using the ROI manager, you can later go back to the images and
> determine which organoid corresponds to which values on the organoid
> excel chart.)

5. By selecting an organoid from the list and clicking Measure, ImageJ
 > will calculate the area of the organoid for you.

 a. area = size of the ROI/spheroid, unit um\^2

 b. Measure -\> results -\> summarize to get the mean, SD, min and
 > max values of the area

 i. check that they are correct in excel

6. When you have charted all the organoids from one field according to
 > steps 3-5, copy the results and add them to the excel sheet (both
 > the basic one and the one for importing the data to R, if you want
 > to use R for visualizing the results). It might also be good to
 > save the ImageJ result table in .csv format to your computer, in
 > case something happens to the excel.

7. Remember to also save the image with the ROIs as a new file. This
 > way you can go back to your ROIs in the future and still keep the

> case something happens to the excel.

7. Remember to also save the image with the ROIs as a new file. This
 > way you can go back to your ROIs in the future and still keep the
 > original image, as it might be needed in the future.

8. Import the results of one image and clear the ROI manager before you
 > start analyzing a new image.

9. Do this to all 3-4 fields of each condition and calculate average
 > areas of organoids for each field, and then for all fields of this
 > condition.

 a. Quantifications of spheroid number and size:

 i. number spheroids per well vs. condition

 ii. spheroid area vs. condition

 b. Growth rate:

 i. spheroid area vs time

 c. Propagation rate?

 i. number of new spheroids vs time

**In addition, I tried performing particle analysis following these
guidelines:**

[[https://imagej.net/imaging/particle-analysis]{.underline}](https://imagej.net/imaging/particle-analysis)

**This approach did not work because adjusting the threshold was
ineffective; spheroids and the background are too similar.**
